stories.title: CRE Comes Together at MADE Bush Terminal for Cocktails and an Exciting New Development
stories.excerpt: On a humid and rainy July night in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, people from throughout the commercial real estate industry escaped the elements at the new mixed-use complex MADE Bush Terminal, which hosted Commercial Observer’s annual summer networking mixer. Amid a series of massive square wooden columns conveying the complex’s industrial feel, attendees networked, enjoyed drinks and small bites, and heard from some of MADE’s pioneering tenants on the benefits of taking space at the complex, which, in addition to sites like a brewery and a new 1,000-capacity music venue, will mostly host companies with light industrial uses. Bridget Chansakul, assistant vice president at the New York City Economic Development Corporation (NYCEDC), handles leasing for the development, and was excited for people to see the goings-on at Building A, where the event took place. While several tenants are already in place in that building, as well as in the project’s Building B, the full development and unveiling of MADE Bush Terminal, which will eventually be a five-building complex, will occur slowly over the next few years. “We’re giving a sneak peek tonight, but it’s just the beginning of what’s happening here in Sunset Park,” said Chansakul. “We’re getting ready to announce a new brewery coming to the site and we’re going to be unveiling a new building, so it’s just the beginning of an exciting and much larger project.” After brief introductions from Brian Pascus, a finance reporter at Commercial Observer, and Jennifer Brown, senior vice president for the Sunset Park district at NYCEDC, attendees were treated to a brief discussion for a deeper introduction to MADE Bush Terminal. Waverly Neer, vice president of asset management at NYCEDC, held a short panel discussion with two of the building’s earliest tenants: lighting manufacturer Stickbulb and design studio Pelle. Pelle, run by husband and wife architect team Jean and Oliver Pelle, moved into Building
bullets: MADE Bush Terminal, Sunset Park Brooklyn, hosts mixed-use industrial/creative tenants.
Event venue signals demand for flexible light-industrial space in Brooklyn.
No direct multifamily component disclosed; primarily office, retail, hospitality focus.
stories.summary: MADE Bush Terminal, a mixed-use complex in Sunset Park, Brooklyn, opened a 1,000-capacity music venue and hosts light industrial and creative tenants. The NYCEDC manages leasing for the property. This signals strong tenant demand for adaptive-reuse industrial space in outer-borough Brooklyn.
stories.impact: Minimal direct multifamily impact. However, the successful leasing and activation of industrial/creative space in Sunset Park indicates broader neighborhood momentum that could support future residential development, particularly mixed-use projects targeting younger professionals and creative workers seeking proximity to Brooklyn's emerging employment clusters.

stories.title: Stetson’s Law School, Bromley Partnering on Tampa Mixed-Use Venture
stories.excerpt: Stetson University plans to modernize the Stetson University College of Law’s Tampa Law Center and create a mixed-use space in a currently underutilized area with the help of Bromley Companies. Bromley hopes to maintain Stetson Law’s presence but add hotel rooms, apartments, restaurants and more. On the 6.5-acre site, Bromley envisions 664 multifamily units, 440,000 square feet of office space, 37,450 square feet of retail space and 300 hotel keys The developer is aiming for a late 2027 groundbreaking. The redevelopment will occur in phases, with no disruption to Stetson’s classes or operation on the property. The university filed a rezoning application to make way for the project, which will require approval from Tampa City Council. The University will retain ownership of the Stetson Law Center and have permanent access to use of the hotel and parking garage. The post Stetson’s Law School, Bromley Partnering on Tampa Mixed-Use Venture appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Stetson University College of Law Tampa, Bromley Companies planning 664 multifamily units, 300 hotel rooms.
6.5-acre redevelopment with late 2027 groundbreaking; rezoning approval pending.
Mixed-use phased development preserves law center operations; tax implications for university partner.
stories.summary: Stetson University and Bromley Companies plan a phased mixed-use redevelopment of the Tampa Law Center campus (6.5 acres) featuring 664 multifamily units, 300 hotel keys, 440,000 sq ft office, and 37,450 sq ft retail. Groundbreaking targeted for late 2027 pending City Council rezoning approval. The university retains ownership of the law center and permanent access to hotel and parking.
stories.impact: Significant supply addition for Tampa multifamily market. The 664-unit project, coupled with institutional partnership providing land/zoning certainty, signals developer confidence in Tampa's education-anchored growth corridor. Permanent hotel/parking use reserves upside value for the university; project enables institutional capital recycling while introducing workforce and market-rate housing near downtown employment.

stories.title: Apartment Developer Pursuing Phillips Place for Multifamily Community
stories.excerpt: Mid-America Apartment Communities is seeking a zoning change to build a 275-unit apartment complex and 15,000 square feet of commercial space at Phillips Place in SouthPark. The Charlotte Business Journal reports the apartment community would be built where the former Regal Cinemas used to stand. The movie theater was demolished in 2022. Phillips Place, a 134,000-square-foot mixed-use center, was built by Lincoln Property Co. and opened in 1997. Phillips Place is now owned by Simon Property Group Inc. The national retail developer acquired the property in November 2025 from Lincoln and The Village Collection for $144.8 million. MAA owns and operates more than 300 properties in 16 states and Washington, D.C. The firm entered the Charlotte market in 2010. Its local portfolio now includes more than 20 properties across the region. The post Apartment Developer Pursuing Phillips Place for Multifamily Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Mid-America Apartment Communities seeking rezoning for 275-unit development at Phillips Place, Charlotte SouthPark.
Simon Property Group acquired mixed-use center November 2025 for $144.8M; former Regal Cinemas site.
MAA has 20+ properties across Charlotte region; conversion signals decline in traditional retail demand.
stories.summary: Mid-America Apartment Communities is pursuing a zoning change to build 275 apartments and 15,000 sq ft retail at Phillips Place in Charlotte's SouthPark submarket, on the site of a demolished Regal Cinemas. Simon Property Group acquired the 134,000-sq-ft mixed-use property in November 2025 for $144.8M. MAA has operated 20+ Charlotte-area properties since 2010.
stories.impact: Strong signal of multifamily conversion appetite in Charlotte's premier retail corridor. Simon's acquisition and MAA's zoning push suggest traditional retail assets in high-traffic mixed-use centers are becoming development targets for residential. This de facto retail-to-residential conversion indicates demand strength in Charlotte SouthPark and SPG's openness to mixed-use repositioning—key for other retail-heavy assets across the sunbelt.

stories.title: S2 Capital Offloads 229-Unit Plano Rental Community
stories.excerpt: Newmark arranged the sale and financing of Hathaway at Willow Bend, a 229-unit multifamily community located at 2525 Preston Road in Plano, Texas. Newmark’s Richard Furr, Brian O’Boyle, Jr., Brian Murphy and Jack Forman represented the seller, S2 Capital Acquisitions, in the transaction. Brian Kochan and Michael Caglianone arranged acquisition financing on behalf of the buyer, Granite Towers Equity Group.  Located along Preston Road in West Plano, Hathaway at Willow Bend is a garden-style community offering one-, two- and three-bedroom residences, including a variety of townhome floor plans. Amenities include two swimming pools, an outdoor kitchen, bark park, natural creek setting and fitness center. Apartment interiors include gourmet kitchens and wood-burning fireplaces.  According to Newmark Research, the Dallas-Fort Worth multifamily market continues to benefit from strong demographic and employment fundamentals. Dallas-Fort Worth led the nation in employment growth from 2019 through 2026, adding more than 604,400 jobs, a 16.3% increase. Connect Texas Multifamily in Dallas on August 13 brings together the owners, developers, brokers, investors, and lenders shaping the future of Texas multifamily for a day of market intelligence, networking, and actionable insights. Join 300+ decision-makers and be part of the conversations driving the Texas multifamily market. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post S2 Capital Offloads 229-Unit Plano Rental Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: S2 Capital sells Hathaway at Willow Bend, 229-unit Plano garden-style community to Granite Towers Equity Group.
DFW added 604,400 jobs (16.3% growth) 2019–2026; leads nation in employment growth.
Strong fundamentals cited; acquisition financing arranged by Newmark; market remains active.
stories.summary: S2 Capital sold Hathaway at Willow Bend, a 229-unit garden-style multifamily property in West Plano, Texas, to Granite Towers Equity Group. Newmark arranged the sale and acquisition financing. DFW employment grew 16.3% (604,400 jobs added) from 2019–2026, leading the nation.
stories.impact: Portfolio secondary-market exit in a fundamentally strong DFW submarket. The sale reflects seller's likely capital recycling; continued buyer appetite in West Plano signals sustained multifamily health despite rising rates, underpinned by DFW's dominant employment growth. This supports development feasibility across DFW metros—active acquisition market reduces sponsorship exit risk and sustains lending.

stories.title: NYC Multifamily Sales Reflect “Two Very Different Markets” in Q2
stories.excerpt: New York City multifamily sales totaled $2.46 billion across 298 transactions in the second quarter of 2026, representing a 25% year-over-year increase in dollar volume but 4% decline in transaction volume compared to Q2 2025, Ariel Property Advisors reported. The number of properties traded was down 11% Y-O-Y to 367. “What we are seeing in the first half of 2026 is a tale of two very different markets, both presenting unique opportunities,” said Shimon Shkury, president and founder of Ariel. “On one side, Manhattan’s free-market sector is showing exceptional strength, rents are up 10% year-over-year, and high-conviction buyers are writing significantly larger checks for supply-constrained assets. “On the other side, rent-stabilized assets are undergoing a massive, painful valuation reset amidst rising expenses and refinancings at double their original rate. Longtime rent-stabilized owners are exiting, and a new wave of buyers is stepping in to acquire these properties at steep discounts. Meanwhile, affordable housing remains a resilient safe haven, drawing deep institutional demand due to its stable, government-backed income.” Manhattan below 96th Street fared best in Q2 with a 315% increase Y-O-Y in dollar volume and 21% increase in transaction volume. Conversely, Brooklyn, the Bronx and Queens all saw double-digit declines in dollar volume. The post NYC Multifamily Sales Reflect “Two Very Different Markets” in Q2 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: NYC multifamily Q2 2026: $2.46B (298 transactions), up 25% YoY volume, down 4% transaction count.
Manhattan free-market rents up 10% YoY; rent-stabilized valuations reset downward, refinancing at 2x original rates.
Brooklyn, Bronx, Queens saw double-digit dollar-volume declines; affordable housing remains resilient institutional demand.
stories.summary: NYC multifamily Q2 2026 sales reached $2.46B across 298 transactions (up 25% YoY in dollars, down 4% in count). Manhattan free-market rents grew 10% YoY with strong high-conviction buyer activity; Manhattan below 96th Street saw 315% YoY dollar-volume increase. Rent-stabilized assets face steep valuation resets amid rising expenses and refinancings at double original rates. Brooklyn, Bronx, and Queens declined double-digit YoY on dollars.
stories.impact: Bifurcated market creates distinct development and investment opportunities. Manhattan free-market supply-constrained assets command premiums and likely attract development capital seeking value-create through renovation and rent growth. Rent-stabilized portfolio exits from longtime owners signal institutional buyer opportunity for value-add repositioning (if legal/regulatory path exists). Outer-borough weakness suggests softer demand fundamentals—developers should focus on cost control and affordability/mixed-income products in Brooklyn/Bronx/Queens to anchor feasibility.

stories.title: Dallas Development Duo Opens 168-Unit Mixed-Income Community
stories.excerpt: Bonner Carrington and Sycamore Development opened Cypress Creek Montfort Drive, a mixed-income community of 168 apartment homes in North Dallas. Located at 14119 Montfort Drive, Cypress Creek Montfort Drive features 116 apartment homes that are income-restricted for residents earning between 30% and 80% of the area median income (AMI), with 52 apartment homes offered at market rate. One-, two-, and three-bedroom apartment homes are available with private balconies. Community amenities include a fitness center, an outdoor grilling station by the pool, a business center with private work pods, and a pet spa. Financing for the community was made possible through the collaboration of multiple public and private partners, including: ·     Federal 9% Low-Income Housing Tax Credit equity syndicated by Hunt Capital Partners, awarded by the Texas Department of Housing and Community Affairs  ·     Construction and permanent financing from East West Bank  ·     A soft loan from the City of Dallas  The community was built by Moss Construction, with Arrive Architecture Group serving as architect. Join the region’s leading multifamily owners, developers, investors, lenders, and brokers at Connect Texas Multifamily on August 13 in Dallas for a focused discussion on the market forces redefining Texas real estate. Be in the room with the decision-makers driving deals and growth across Texas. Register today: www.ConnectTXMF2026.com. The post Dallas Development Duo Opens 168-Unit Mixed-Income Community appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Cypress Creek Montfort Drive opens 168 units North Dallas: 116 affordable (30-80% AMI), 52 market-rate.
9% LIHTC equity, East West Bank financing, City of Dallas soft loan; mixed-income model proven.
Dallas public-private partnerships enabling workforce housing; tax-credit structure de-risks affordable development.
stories.summary: Bonner Carrington and Sycamore Development opened Cypress Creek Montfort Drive, a 168-unit mixed-income community in North Dallas with 116 affordable units (30–80% AMI) and 52 market-rate units. Financing included 9% LIHTC equity (Hunt Capital Partners), East West Bank construction/permanent loans, and a Dallas soft loan. The property offers one-, two-, and three-bedroom units and full amenities.
stories.impact: Model demonstrates viability of dense mixed-income multifamily in Dallas with layered public-private capital. The 9% LIHTC structure, municipal soft debt, and private construction/perm financing show how developers can build workforce housing at scale in supply-constrained sunbelt markets. This template is replicable across Dallas and other high-growth metros facing affordability pressure; success signals city willingness to deploy soft capital for housing, reducing sponsor equity need and improving returns on affordable-focused development.

stories.title: Logistics Firm Highline Commerce Grows to 60K SF at Industry City, Thanks to Robots
stories.excerpt: Third-party logistics provider Highline Commerce is expanding its industrial footprint at Brooklyn’s Industry City for the second time in four years, Commercial Observer has learned. Highline’s lease renewal at 67 34th Street in Brooklyn’s Sunset Park neighborhood tacked on another 18,000 square feet to its warehouse space. The five-year deal rounds out the company’s total footprint at the mixed-use commercial campus to 60,000 square feet. The Brooklyn-based logistics company specializes in fulfilling orders for early-stage e-commerce brands, according to its website. Highline credits its growth, in part, to neighborly robots. The company has teamed up with fellow Industry City tenant Ultra Robotics to install “humanoid robots” that are fulfilling up to 30 percent of Highline’s clients’ orders, according to an Industry City spokesperson. The 60,000-square-foot flexible industrial space will remain largely dedicated to fulfillment operations, along with some office space and social rooms. Highline Commerce negotiated the deal directly with Industry City’s Jeff Fein and Brad Blum, who represent the landlord in-house. Asking rent was $28 per square foot. Highline arrived at Industry City from Manhattan’s Chelsea neighborhood in 2022. During its initial four-year lease, the company’s warehouse expanded from 27,000 square feet to 42,000 square feet. Highline plans to remain at Industry City as long as it can keep growing. “New York is in the middle of an e-commerce golden age, and brands are launching faster than the logistics world can support them,” Richard Hurley, founder and CEO of Highline, said in a statement. “Serving them doesn’t just take a village, it takes a place like Industry City, where on-site manufacturing, printing, fulfillment and the robots that run it all operate within feet of one another.” Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Highline Commerce expands to 60,000 sq ft at Industry City Brooklyn, adding 18,000 sq ft in five-year renewal.
E-commerce 3PL uses humanoid robots (Ultra Robotics tenant partnership) for 30% of fulfillment; automation drives occupancy retention.
Industrial submarket benefiting from logistics/automation tenant stickiness in mixed-use setting.
stories.summary: Highline Commerce, a Brooklyn-based third-party logistics provider, renewed and expanded its lease at Industry City in Sunset Park to 60,000 sq ft total (adding 18,000 sq ft over five years). The company leverages humanoid robots (partnered with fellow tenant Ultra Robotics) to fulfill approximately 30% of client orders. The space houses fulfillment, office, and social areas.
stories.impact: Demonstrates underlying strength of Brooklyn's mixed-use industrial ecosystem and the durability of last-mile logistics tenancy. Automation adoption by high-growth 3PLs justifies both renewal and expansion, suggesting robust demand for flexible industrial space in waterfront/transit-proximate submarkets. For multifamily developers near industrial clusters (e.g., Sunset Park, Williamsburg waterfront), this signals strong job/income stability in adjacent residential catchments, supporting rent growth and absorption assumptions.

stories.title: Bakery Shop Cafe Aroma to Open New Spot on the Upper East Side
stories.excerpt: Time to rise and grind, Upper East Siders, as a new coffee shop and bakery is set to open at Avenues Equity Partners’ 1410 Madison Avenue in Carnegie Hill. Family-owned coffee shop and bakery Cafe Aroma has taken 2,200 square feet of retail space at the base of the five-story residential building, according to landlord broker Meridian Capital Group. The asking rent and the length of the lease were not disclosed. The average asking rent for retail space on the Upper East Side was $252 per square foot during the second quarter of 2026, according to data from CBRE. Meridian’s Jacob Mayer represented the landlord in this deal, while Shai Baradarian and Tamir Baradarian of eRealty Advisors represented Cafe Aroma. The Baradarians did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. “1410 Madison Avenue is a rare turnkey restaurant space in a high-traffic Upper East Side location, and it generated strong interest from operators looking to be near Mount Sinai and Carnegie Hill’s dense residential base,” Mayer said in a statement. “Cafe Aroma is a great fit for the space, and we’re pleased to have completed a lease that positions both the landlord and the tenant for long-term success in the corridor.” This will be Cafe Aroma’s sixth Manhattan location and its first on the Upper East Side. Avenues Equity Partners acquired 1410 Madison Avenue and 1412 Madison Avenue for $6.3 million in January 2025, according to property records. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Cafe Aroma opens 2,200 sq ft retail space at 1410 Madison Avenue, Upper East Side; landlord Avenues Equity Partners.
Manhattan retail comps: $252/sq ft asking rent Q2 2026; high-traffic residential-anchored location near Mount Sinai.
Ground-floor retail activation in residential building; F&B tenant signals consumer confidence in UES retail.
stories.summary: Cafe Aroma, a family-owned coffee shop and bakery, leased 2,200 sq ft of retail space at 1410 Madison Avenue in Manhattan's Carnegie Hill (Upper East Side) at the base of a five-story residential building owned by Avenues Equity Partners. The area's average retail asking rent was $252/sq ft in Q2 2026. The deal was brokered by Meridian Capital Group and eRealty Advisors.
stories.impact: Minimal direct development impact. However, successful ground-floor F&B leasing in an Upper East Side residential building underscores the viability and resilience of integrated retail-residential mixed-use in Manhattan's prime neighborhoods. For multifamily developers planning ground-floor activations in Manhattan, this signals tenant interest in high-traffic residential nodes and justifies retail hold-ready units or flexible ground-floor designs.

stories.title: Hudson Bay Capital Supplies $60M Refi for Redeveloped N.J. Office Campus
stories.excerpt: A joint venture between Somerset Development and HIG Capital has sealed a $60 million loan to refinance a repurposed New Jersey mixed-use office campus that will soon be home to sandwich chain Jersey’s Mike’s headquarters, Commercial Observer has learned. Hudson Bay Capital provided the bridge loan for the sponsorship’s 276,900-square-foot property Bell Works Fort Monmouth property in Tinton Falls, N.J. Commvault, a data protection firm, developed the property as its headquarters in 2014 before selling it to Somerset last year as part of a plan to reimagine the site as flexible office and retail space. “By reimagining an underutilized suburban campus as a vibrant mixed-use destination, we’ve created lasting value for investors, tenants and the community,” Ralph Zucker, president and CEO of Inspired by Somerset Development, said in a statement. “This financing reinforces Bell Works’ role as a catalyst for the continued revitalization of Fort Monmouth.” Located at 1 CommVault Way near where Netflix is building a $1 billion studio complex, Bell Works Fort Monmouth has inked 222,000 square feet of leases since Somerset redeveloped it, the website TAPinto Middletown reported last month. Jersey Mike’s signed a 72,326-square-foot lease to relocate its corporate headquarters there from Manasquan, N.J., and also leased a 2,286 square-foot retail location. The deals bring the property’s occupancy to 88 percent, according to the publication. Commvault, which formerly was the sole tenant of Bell Works, has maintained its headquarters at the campus and occupies 101,878 square feet. Walker & Dunlop negotiated the debt with a capital markets institutional advisory team consisting of Dustin Stolly, Aaron Appel, Sean Reimer, Keith Kurland, Jonathan Schwartz, Adam Schwartz, Ari Hirt and Stanley Cayre. “Bell Works Fort Monmouth is a highly differentiated mixed-use campus that continues to demonstrate strong leasing momentum and long-term value creation,” Stolly, senior
bullets: Hudson Bay Capital $60M bridge refi for Bell Works Fort Monmouth, 276,900 sq ft mixed-use office/retail campus.
Jersey Mike's HQ relocation tenant anchor; Netflix $1B studio complex development nearby Fort Monmouth.
Suburban office redevelopment as mixed-use destination amid office decline; public-asset repurposing catalyst for regional revival.
stories.summary: A joint venture between Somerset Development and HIG Capital secured a $60M bridge loan from Hudson Bay Capital to refinance Bell Works Fort Monmouth, a 276,900-sq-ft mixed-use office campus in Tinton Falls, N.J. Jersey Mike's sandwich chain is relocating its headquarters to the property. Commvault originally developed the site as its HQ in 2014; Somerset acquired it last year to repurpose as flexible office and retail. Netflix is building a $1B studio complex nearby.
stories.impact: Signals successful suburban office-to-mixed-use conversion and private-sector anchoring via corporate HQ relocation. Jersey Mike's move signals confidence in Fort Monmouth submarket revival. Netflix's nearby $1B studio investment and HQ tenant migration create employment clustering that could support multifamily development on adjacent or underutilized suburban parcels. Developers should scout Fort Monmouth-area land for workforce housing targeting office/tech/media workers in this emerging secondary market.

stories.title: New Empire Corporation Buys Midtown Parking Garage for $33M
stories.excerpt: Bentley Zhao’s New Empire Corporation, a New York City-based developer of residential mixed-use buildings, dropped $33.5 million to acquire a Midtown parking garage at 10 East 30th Street, according to a deed filed in property records on Thursday. The purchase, made through the entity Rolls Yacht Tower Management, follows an application filed by the developer last week with the New York City Department of Buildings to demolish the seven-story, 48,500-square-foot garage. Zhao has yet to disclose his exact plans for the demolition site, but New Empire’s website advertises the address as part of the company’s “Manhattan expansion.” Roughly four blocks east, at 567 Second Avenue, New Empire is planning a 14-story luxury condominium building, according to the website. A spokesman for New Empire did not respond to a request for comment. The deal was made with a $20 million mortgage from Ponce Bank. It is unclear whether either party in the purchase worked with outside brokerages. The seller, Aero Parking, has operated the parking garage between Madison and Fifth avenues since 1995, according to property records. New Empire recently entered into a contract to acquire another Midtown development, this one a seven-story office property at 4 West 43rd Street. The property was advertised as an office-to-residential redevelopment opportunity. A representative for Aero Parking did not respond to a request for comment. Emily Davis can be reached at edavis@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: New Empire Corporation acquires Midtown parking garage at 10 East 30th Street for $33.5M; demolition application filed.
Ponce Bank provided $20M mortgage; deal undisclosed use, marketed as Manhattan expansion site.
Parking-to-residential conversion play; New Empire planning luxury condo 567 Second Avenue nearby.
stories.summary: New Empire Corporation acquired a seven-story, 48,500-sq-ft Midtown parking garage at 10 East 30th Street for $33.5M and immediately filed for demolition permits with NYC DOB. A $20M mortgage from Ponce Bank financed the deal. The developer has not disclosed redevelopment plans but markets the site as part of its Manhattan expansion; New Empire is also developing a 14-story luxury condo at 567 Second Avenue (four blocks east).
stories.impact: Opportunistic acquisition of underutilized parking asset by residential developer signals continued tightness in Manhattan developable land. New Empire's rapid pivot to demolition suggests high confidence in residential-use entitlements or market demand in the Kips Bay/Murray Hill submarket. The $33.5M acquisition price for 48,500 sq ft (~$690/sq ft) implies developer expects significant upside from residential conversion, indicating strong market fundamentals or zoning allowance for higher density/residential use in Midtown—watch for similar parking conversions as Manhattan zoning continues to shift toward housing.

stories.title: Musk’s companies prep $5M Seaholm office renovation
stories.excerpt: Downtown Austin’s Seaholm Power Plant, which was quietly occupied by several Elon Musk-affiliated companies earlier this year, is getting a $5.2 million facelift.  The planned renovations have been put in place by Wit Tech, a company with links to SpaceX, the firm that is pushing the construction of the Terafab semiconductor manufacturing plant in Grimes County. Musk’s companies occupy 35,000 square feet of space at 800 West Cesar Chavez Street, according to the Austin American Statesman. Musk is typically bombastic about his companies’ pursuits and ambitious, but has not yet publicly commented on the office space. He has given notes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Musk-affiliated companies occupying 35,000 sq ft Seaholm Power Plant downtown Austin; $5.2M office renovation underway.
SpaceX/Wit Tech driving Terafab semiconductor fab in Grimes County; $55B project, 100M sq ft potential, tax breaks secured.
Austin major employer expansion; office renovation signals company permanence; regional impact on housing demand, labor clustering.
stories.summary: Multiple Elon Musk-affiliated companies occupy 35,000 sq ft at Seaholm Power Plant (800 West Cesar Chavez, downtown Austin) and are undertaking a $5.2M renovation via Wit Tech (SpaceX-linked). Musk's Terafab semiconductor manufacturing project in Grimes County (40+ miles north) is planned at $55B with potential scaling to 100M sq ft. Grimes County approved tax breaks and a tax increment reinvestment zone (TIRZ).
stories.impact: Major employment anchor formation on a multi-year horizon. Terafab's $55B scale and potential 100M-sq-ft footprint would drive significant in-migration of engineering, manufacturing, and support-services workforce to central Texas. Downtown Austin office demand and Grimes County land development create parallel housing demand vectors: downtown professionals and suburban/exurban manufacturing workforce. Developers should accelerate land acquisition in Austin metro (downtown, north Austin, Grimes County corridor) and model workforce housing, mixed-income product, and master-planned communities ahead of phase-one Terafab buildout (likely 2027+).

stories.title: Florida’s largest Live Local Act project is bold. Its ambitious developer is controversial
stories.excerpt: Pablo Castro came into South Florida real estate with a bang. Over the past two years, the Spanish developer, who has never built in the U.S. and until recently had zero name recognition here, proposed Florida’s largest known multifamily complex, bought the site, secured approval and stirred controversy: Castro faces lawsuits by three local industry players who say they were stiffed after playing a role in laying the groundwork for the project.  HueHub would consist of 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres in West Little River. It is planned under the state’s Live Local Act, which allows […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Pablo Castro (Spanish developer) proposing HueHub: 4,040 units, 7 towers, 12 acres West Little River Miami; Live Local Act.
All units furnished, attainably priced; exceeds 40% affordable requirement; amenities free to residents.
Developer faces three lawsuits alleging non-payment to local industry partners; unproven US track record raises execution risk.
stories.summary: Spanish developer Pablo Castro is building HueHub, Florida's largest known multifamily project: 4,040 units across seven 34-story towers on 12 acres in West Little River, Miami, under the state's Live Local Act. All units will be furnished and attainably priced (exceeding the 40% affordable requirement); free amenities include kitchens, learning center, fitness studios. Castro secured land and approvals but faces three lawsuits alleging he stiffed local industry partners; he has no prior U.S. development experience.
stories.impact: High-risk, high-reward supply injection into Miami market. HueHub's 4,040 units represent significant new competitive supply; the furnished, all-affordable model may set market precedent and rental-rate pressure in West Little River/Wynwood corridor. Castro's litigation and track-record vacuum create uncertainty around delivery timing and ultimate absorption. Competing developers should monitor HueHub's leasing velocity and unit economics closely—if successful, it validates all-affordable furnished model and may drive competitive response; if delayed/troubled, it signals sponsor-execution risk and potential supply-pipeline volatility in Miami. Existing Miami multifamily owners should stress-test rents and occupancy assumptions for HueHub phase-in (likely 2027+).

stories.title: Faena Residences developers presell $30M penthouse for record price along Miami River
stories.excerpt: Shahab Karmely and Edgardo Defortuna’s joint venture presold a penthouse at their Faena-branded luxury condo development along the Miami River for $30 million, a record for the area on price as well as price per square foot.  Karmely’s KAR Properties has long planned to develop the waterfront site into the Rafael Viñoly-designed project. In 2022, Karmely brought on Defortuna’s Fortune International Group as a partner. Last year, they launched the Faena Residences.  A mystery buyer is under contract to buy a penthouse in the east tower that will span 8,200 square feet of interior space and also include 7,000 square […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Faena Residences Miami River presold penthouse for $30M (~$3,700/sq ft); 8,200 sq ft interior, 7,000 sq ft outdoor.
KAR Properties and Fortune International Group joint venture; Rafael Viñoly architecture; Swire's Mandarin Residences comparable ($6,300/sq ft when built).
Luxury pre-sales momentum signals strong high-end condo demand and financing availability in Miami waterfront.
stories.summary: KAR Properties and Fortune International Group's Faena Residences (Miami River) presold a penthouse duplex for $30M (~$3,700/sq ft interior) with 8,200 sq ft interior and 7,000 sq ft outdoor space including private rooftop pool. The project is designed by Rafael Viñoly. Comparable luxury pre-sales include Swire's Mandarin Residences (targeting ~$6,300/sq ft upon 2030 completion) and Witkoff/Monroe Capital's Shore Club Miami Beach ($120M+ penthouse).
stories.impact: Demonstrates robust pre-construction luxury condo demand and financing in Miami's prime waterfront submarkets. Pre-sales at these price points ($30M–$120M) signal deep institutional and UHNW buyer demand ahead of groundbreaking—critical for sponsor equity and construction financing confidence. For mid-market multifamily developers, this signals affluent residential demand in Miami but also highlights concentration of capital at ultra-luxury tiers. Watch: if luxury condo pre-sales slow, it may indicate broader financing tightness or cap-rate compression limiting sponsor returns; conversely, strong waterfront sales may absorb wealthy buyer demand and leave upper-middle-market renter pools relatively uncontested.

stories.title: LA city’s top construction permits: Week ending July 11, 2026
stories.excerpt: 🏆 The largest development to receive permits last week was for a single-family build in Encino, where plans were filed for 11,278 square feet of work across two permits at 3864 North Kim Lane. The project includes a new two-story single-family dwelling with an attached three-car garage and a pond. Job Filing Numbers: 25010-10000-05697, 25010-10000-05874, 25047-10000-01751 and more 📊 In Valley Glen, permits were issued for 8,765 square feet of construction across three permits at 14414-14418 1/2 West Tiara Street. The project includes building a new accessory dwelling unit, along with a three-story duplex. The work is estimated to cost […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: LA permits week ending July 11, 2026: single-family (11,278 sq ft Encino), ADU/duplex (8,765 sq ft Valley Glen).
Alteration permits: $350K+ for Bel Air megamansion 'The One' (Richard Saghian, Fashion Nova owner, $141M purchase 2022).
Single-family, ADU focus; limited multifamily permit activity reported; market tilted toward ultra-high-end residential and detached housing.
stories.summary: LA building permits issued week of July 11, 2026, included a single-family home in Encino (11,278 sq ft with attached garage and pond), an ADU and three-story duplex in Valley Glen (8,765 sq ft, ~$880K cost), and a major alteration to Bel Air's 'The One' megamansion (75,856 sq ft, $350K+ budget). Richard Saghian (Fashion Nova owner) purchased The One in 2022 for $141M.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily signal. LA permit flow shows continued ultra-luxury single-family, ADU, and ultra-high-end alterations; absence of large multifamily permits suggests either (1) permit batching/lag, (2) slow new entitlements, or (3) developer focus on pipeline clearance rather than new starts. For multifamily sponsors, this underscores LA's zoning-constrained development environment and the value of entitled land. Track multi-family permit trends monthly; sustained low multifamily issuance despite strong metro demand indicates supply-side bottleneck that supports rents but limits development volume.

stories.title: Jed Walentas, Tom Brady’s ex-manager abruptly settle legal battle over Bahamas mansion
stories.excerpt: A game of catch-me-if-you-can between Jed Walentas and Tom Brady’s former manager over a hurricane-damaged Bahamas mansion has come to an abrupt end. The Two Trees Management CEO had been trying to collect a $300,000 arbitration award from Will McDonough since last July, but repeatedly failed to locate him for formal legal service, court filings show. On Monday, Walentas notched a win when Manhattan Supreme Court Justice Kathleen Waterman-Marshall authorized Walentas to serve McDonough via email and set October 27 as the date McDonough or his legal team must show up at court to explain why he shouldn’t be forced […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Jed Walentas (Two Trees Management CEO) and Will McDonough (Tom Brady's ex-manager) settle Bahamas mansion dispute; $300K arbitration award.
Bunkers Investments LLC partnership (2015): $4.9M combined capital; hurricane damage required major capital infusion beyond insurance.
Partnership/litigation resolved abruptly via email service; outcome (payment vs. settlement) unclear from court filing.
stories.summary: Jed Walentas (Two Trees Management CEO) and Will McDonough (Tom Brady's former manager) settled a legal dispute over a hurricane-damaged luxury rental mansion at Bakers Bay, Bahamas. Walentas had been pursuing a ~$300K arbitration award since July 2025. The partners formed Bunkers Investments LLC in 2015 with combined capital of $4.9M ($2.45M Walentas, $750K + $1.7M credits McDonough). Hurricane damage required substantial capital beyond insurance payouts.
stories.impact: Minimal multifamily development impact. Story reflects underlying risk in luxury resort/vacation-rental development partnerships: insurance inadequacy post-disaster, capital calls, and partnership dissolution. For multifamily sponsors with resort or second-home product lines, note: catastrophic event capital requirements and partner-dispute resolution timelines are material underwriting variables. Avoid overleveraged luxury-rental partnerships without robust insurance, buyout, or dissolution mechanisms.

stories.title: Fort Worth Stockyards will get $71M apartment project as $1B planned redevelopment hangs in balance
stories.excerpt: Oldham Goodwin Group and Fort Worth Housing Solutions broke ground on The Exchange, a $71 million, 296-unit affordable apartment complex north of Fort Worth’s historic Stockyards. The apartments are a small slice of a 14.6-acre mixed-use development from Oldham Goodwin aimed at enhancing the Stockyards. The apartment complex features affordable units in partnership with Fort Worth Housing Solutions, and is slated to be the residential portion of the wider development. Amenities include a pool, clubhouse and a dog park. The apartment sizes begin at studio sizes and scale to three-bedroom units, according to the Dallas Business Journal.  Stockyards North, which […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Fort Worth Stockyards: Oldham Goodwin Group and Fort Worth Housing Solutions break ground on The Exchange, $71M, 296-unit affordable complex.
Mixed-income: all units 30-80% AMI with workforce/affordable subsidy; Stockyards North phase adds hotel, retail, dining walkable district.
Wider $1B phase-two renovation mired in legal dispute; first phase successful; housing first in new North district.
stories.summary: Oldham Goodwin Group and Fort Worth Housing Solutions broke ground on The Exchange, a $71M, 296-unit mixed-income apartment complex north of Fort Worth's historic Stockyards. All units serve residents at 30–80% AMI. The project is the residential anchor for Stockyards North, a broader 14.6-acre mixed-use district planned to include a Home2Suites hotel, retail, and dining on 29th and North Main. A wider $1B phase-two Stockyards renovation is currently in legal dispute.
stories.impact: Validates public-private affordable-housing model in Texas mid-market and signals tourism-adjacent workforce housing opportunity. The Exchange's all-affordable model (vs. mixed-income) and placement at entry to historic district indicate city/partner commitment to place-making and workforce retention. Success here could anchor broader Stockyards North leasing and justify follow-on phases. For developers in secondary Texas metros with heritage districts, this demonstrates feasibility of affordable housing as revitalization catalyst; investigate municipal soft debt, LIHTC, and public-land partnerships as deal enablers.
